title: Choosing k: Small vs. Large Neighborhoods
concept: The tradeoff between small k (sensitive to outliers) and large k (boundary smoothing), and using odd numbers to prevent ties.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between Manhattan distance and straight-line distance?

options.text: Manhattan distance measures paths along grid lines (up and across), while straight-line distance measures a direct diagonal path.
Manhattan distance is always shorter than straight-line distance.
Straight-line distance can only be used on actual physical maps.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Manhattan distance is grid-constrained, while straight-line distance is a direct diagonal shortcut.

cards.title: Setting the boundary
cards.body: If you want to know what a cool trend is, do you ask just 1 person, or do you take a poll of 50 people? Asking 1 person is quick but risky (they might have weird tastes). Asking 50 people is safer but takes longer, and you might lose the local detail. Choosing the number of neighbors (k) in kNN is the exact same tradeoff!

cards.title: Small vs. large k
cards.body: Choosing k determines the size of the neighborhood. A small k (like k = 1) looks at tiny details but is easily fooled by noise. A large k (like k = 15) is stable but can wash out small patterns, making the decision boundaries too smooth.

cards.title: Rumors vs. surveys
cards.body: If k = 1, it's like believing the very first rumor you hear. If that single neighbor is incorrect (an outlier), you make a mistake. If k = 15, you survey a crowd. One weird rumor gets outvoted by the majority, giving you a much more reliable answer.

cards.title: No tie-breaker needed
cards.body: If you have 2 categories (Red vs. Blue) and choose an even neighbor count like k = 4, you might get a tie: 2 Red and 2 Blue votes! To avoid this deadlock, we always choose an odd number for k (like 3, 5, or 7) so there is always a clear winner.

cards.title: More neighbors isn't always better
cards.body: You might think choosing the largest possible k is best. But if you have 100 points and set k = 100, every vote will just predict the most common category in the entire dataset, ignoring where the new point actually is! Keep k reasonably small.

cards.title: Comparing neighborhoods
cards.body: How k size affects decision sensitivity:

left.text:
Highly sensitive to outliers.
Jagged boundaries.
Easily fooled by noise.

right.text:
Smooth boundaries.
Filters out noisy points.
May ignore small, real groups.

exercises.prompt: Why is a model with k = 1 highly sensitive to noise or outliers?

options.text: Because it makes its decision based on a single closest point, which might be an error or anomaly.
Because it ignores the closest point entirely.
Because it requires too many votes to make a decision.

exercises.explain: Spot on. If k = 1, a single misplaced data point will completely dictate the prediction for anything near it.

exercises.prompt: True or False: We should choose an odd number for k (like 3 or 5) when we have two categories to avoid voting ties.
exercises.explain: True! An odd number ensures there is always a majority winner and prevents deadlocks.

exercises.prompt: Complete the description of the k-tradeoff:
exercises.sentence: A very ___ value of k makes the model stable but might smooth out important local patterns.
exercises.explain: A large k considers a wider neighborhood, smoothing out noise but potentially washing away smaller distinct groups.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scenarios shows the risk of choosing k = 1?

options.text: An email is classified as spam just because its closest neighbor happens to be a single mislabeled spam email.
An email is classified as spam after taking a vote among 9 nearby emails.
An email is classified as spam because the word 'free' was found in it.

exercises.explain: Yes! Making a decision based on just 1 neighbor makes the system fragile to individual anomalies.

exercises.prompt: You are setting up kNN to identify fake coins. You have a dataset of 100 coins (90 genuine, 10 fake). If you set k = 100, what will the model predict for any new coin you test?

options.text: It will always predict 'genuine' because genuine coins hold the absolute majority (90 out of 100) in the entire dataset.
It will look at the 1 closest neighbor and predict based on that.
It will crash because k cannot be equal to the number of data points.
It will correctly identify fake coins because it checks every single coin.

exercises.explain: Perfect! If k is equal to the total dataset size, the model uses all points for every vote. The majority category (genuine) will win every single time, rendering the coordinates useless! Keep k small relative to the dataset size.
